name: river_microplastic_sludge_fertilizer_policy
premise: A wastewater plant produces biosolids used as cheap fertilizer by regional farms. New testing shows elevated microplastic content and trace industrial chemicals after a nearby factory upgraded packaging lines. Director Sofia Mendes can halt biosolids distribution and send sludge to a landfill at triple cost, which will raise water bills and force some farms to buy expensive alternatives. If she continues distribution under existing permits, microplastics may accumulate in soil and enter food chains, inviting lawsuits and long-term harm. The factory offers to fund improved filtration, but installation takes six months. A local journalist has obtained the test results and plans to publish tomorrow.

agents.name: Director Sofia Mendes
agents.identity: I am the wastewater plant director responsible for biosolids decisions when short-term affordability may hide long-term ecological harm.
agents.goal: I want to protect public health and soil integrity without imposing sudden costs that bankrupt farms and households.

agents.name: Farmer Dana Whitfield
agents.identity: I am a farmer relying on biosolids to keep operations viable when fertilizer markets are volatile.
agents.goal: I want a policy that does not make small farms the shock absorber for industrial contamination we did not create.

choices: I will halt biosolids land application immediately and pursue emergency funding for disposal and filtration, accepting short-term cost spikes to prevent long-term contamination.
I will continue distribution with strict labeling and accelerated filtration planning, accepting contamination risk to avoid immediate economic harm and bill shocks.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Test results are about to go public and farms are planning planting schedules. Director Sofia Mendes must choose between halting biosolids immediately or continuing with mitigation plans under time pressure. What does Director Sofia Mendes do?
